DISCOVERY 301, PLAINTIFF WILLIAM HELTON'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUESTS FOR ADMISSION, SET ONE IS CONTINUED FOR HEARING TO MAY 16, 2025. THE COURT FINDS THAT PLAINTIFF'S FAILURE TO MEET AND CONFER HERE IS A MISUSE OF THE DISCOVERY PROCESS AND ORDERS PLAINTIFF TO PAY $500 TO DEFENDANT IN SANCTIONS WITHIN 10 COURT DAYS OF ENTRY OF THIS ORDER. THE PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO MEET AND CONFER IN PERSON OR TELEPHONICALLY TO NARROW THEIR DISCOVERY DISPUTES. IF ANY DISPUTES REMAIN FOR HEARING, PLAINTIFF SHALL FILE AN UPDATED BRIEF OF NO MORE THAN 10 PAGES AND AN UPDATED SEPARATE STATEMENT AT LEAST 9 COURT DAYS IN ADVANCE OF THE HEARING. DEFENDANT MAY FILE A RESPONDING BRIEF OF NO MORE THAN 10 PAGES AND AN UPDATED SEPARATE STATEMENT AT LEAST FIVE COURT DAYS IN ADVANCE OF THE HEARING. (SEE MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE DETAILS) COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. DISCOVERY 301, PLAINTIFF WILLIAM HELTON'S MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO PLAINTIFF'S REQUEST FOR PRODUCTION OF DOCUMENTS, SET ONE, IS GRANTED IN PART AND CONTINUED IN PART TO MAY 16, 2025. THE COURT FINDS THAT PLAINTIFF'S FAILURE TO MEET AND CONFER HERE IS A MISUSE OF THE DISCOVERY PROCESS AND ORDERS PLAINTIFF TO PAY $500 TO DEFENDANT IN SANCTIONS WITHIN 10 COURT DAYS OF ENTRY OF THIS ORDER. RATHER THAN IMPOSE THE DRASTIC REMEDY OF DENYING FURTHER DISCOVERY ON THIS SET ENTIRELY, THE COURT ORDERS AS FOLLOWS: DEFENDANT SHALL PRODUCE TO PLAINTIFF THE DOCUMENTS REQUIRED TO BE DISCLOSED BY CCP 871.26(H) WITHIN 15 COURT DAYS OF ENTRY OF THIS ORDER. FOLLOWING THIS PRODUCTION, IF PLAINTIFF SEEKS ANY FURTHER PRODUCTION, THE PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO MEET AND CONFER TELEPHONICALLY OR IN PERSON TO NARROW THEIR DISCOVERY DISPUTES. THE BALANCE OF THE MOTION IS CONTINUED FOR HEARING TO MAY 16, 2025. IF ANY DISPUTES REMAIN FOR HEARING, PLAINTIFF SHALL FILE AN UPDATED BRIEF OF NO MORE THAN 10 PAGES AND AN UPDATED SEPARATE STATEMENT AT LEAST 9 COURT DAYS IN ADVANCE OF THE HEARING. PLAINTIFF MUST CARRY ITS BURDEN OF SETTING OUT SPECIFIC FACTS SHOWING GOOD CAUSE JUSTIFYING PRODUCTION OF THE DOCUMENTS IT SEEKS. SEE CCP 2031.310(B)(1). DEFENDANT MAY FILE A RESPONDING BRIEF OF NO MORE THAN 10 PAGES AND AN UPDATED SEPARATE STATEMENT AT LEAST FIVE COURT DAYS IN ADVANCE OF THE HEARING. (SEE MINI MINUTES FOR COMPLETE DETAILS) COUNSEL FOR THE PREVAILING PARTY IS REQUIRED TO PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER WHICH REPEATS VERBATIM THE SUBSTANTIVE PORTION OF THE TENTATIVE RULING IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: CHRISTINE VAN AKEN; CLERK: W. TRUPEK; NOT REPORTED (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One
Off Calendar May-16-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF WILLIAM HELTON's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Request For Production Of Documents, Set One.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

On April 1, 2025, the court ordered the parties to meet and confer and to file supplemental briefs describing any remaining discovery dispute. Plaintiff William Helton's supplemental counsel's declaration seeks further relief in two respects: First, as to all RFPs other than 30 and 31, the Schmitt Declaration states that the parties have agreed to production parameters pending a protective order, which the parties have not yet negotiated. The court orders Hyundai Motor America to produce the documents it has agreed to produce within 20 days of entry of this order. As to any documents Hyundai contends are confidential, it may designate them as confidential in its production. Helton may challenge any confidentiality designation; the parties must meet and confer and the court will resolve any disputes about confidentiality designations upon noticed motion. Helton shall not file any of Hyundai's confidential documents in the public record or use any confidential documents in open court absent leave of the court but may lodge these documents under seal and notify Hyundai so it may make any appropriate sealing motion. Helton shall not share confidential documents outside of its law firm except with the plaintiff himself and any retained experts or consultants, who shall agree to bound by the terms of this protective order before they receive confidential documents. Helton shall return or destroy confidential documents within 60 days of the final conclusion of this litigation.

Helton further seeks an order compelling Hyundai to produce documents responsive to RFP Nos. 30 and 31. Hyundai has not submitted a supplemental brief but briefed this issue extensively in its March 18, 2025 opposition to the motion. The court now turns to this issue.

RFP 30 seeks a list or compilation of other customer complaints that are "substantially similar" to complaints made by Helton about his 2023 Hyundai Santa Cruz vehicle. A very broad definition of substantial similarity is given. RFP 31 seeks "all documents that refer to, reflect, or relate to any Field Service Action issued, or in the process of being issued, in response to complaints experienced by Plaintiff as described in Defendant's warranty history/summary and within the line items of the repair orders created at Defendant's authorized repair facility" [some capitalizations omitted]. The term Field Service Action is not defined. Hyundai objected to both RFPs as unintelligible, overbroad, and irrelevant.

This is a Song-Beverly Act case where Helton is seeking replacement value, damages related to his repair of and inability to use the vehicle, attorneys' fees, and civil penalties for willful violations of the Act. Hyundai's knowledge of the same defects in other 2023 Hyundai Santa Cruz vehicles is relevant to whether any violations here were willful. But Helton's requests are broad and not wholly clear, and Hyundai presents unrebutted evidence that the burden of responding to these RFPs as they are drafted would far exceed their evidentiary value in this case. (Blackburn Dec. paras. 19-30.) The court reforms RFP 30 as follows: Hyundai shall provide a list of instances where a consumer presented a 2023 Hyundai Santa Cruz for repair in California where the vehicle had a seatbelt that failed to latch, a Tonneau cover that failed to lock, or a "Driver Seat Unbuckled" alert that activated when that seatbelt was buckled. Hyundai may redact consumer names for privacy.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling, with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Form Interrogatories, Set One
Off Calendar Apr-01-2025 Continued To May-16-25 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF WILLIAM HELTON'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiff'S Form Interrogatories, Set One is continued for hearing to May 16, 2025.

Plaintiff's meet and confer efforts were inadequate and demonstrate no meaningful effort to narrow the disputes in this case before seeking judicial intervention. Plaintiff sent a lengthy letter that did not meaningfully engage with Defendant's objections, and then did not respond to Defendant's invitation to discuss the parties' discovery disputes. (See Cowden Dec., paras. 7-8.) The court further notes that Plaintiff's discovery requests are extremely broad in the context of a Song-Beverly action. Plaintiff's informal resolution efforts fell far short of what was required. (See Obregon v. Superior Court (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 424, 431-433 [discussion of factors court may consider in deciding whether meet and confer efforts were sufficient].)

It is a misuse of the discovery process to fail to make good faith efforts to narrow disputes before seeking judicial intervention. (CCP 2023.010(i).) CCP 2023.020 provides, "Notwithstanding the outcome of the particular discovery motion, the court shall impose a monetary sanction ordering that any party or attorney who fails to confer as required pay the reasonable expenses, including attorney's fees, incurred by anyone as a result of that conduct." The court finds that Plaintiff's failure to meet and confer here is a misuse of the discovery process and orders Plaintiff to pay $500 to Defendant in sanctions within 10 court days of entry of this order.

However, the court recognizes its discretion in selecting a remedy for a failure to meet and confer. (Obregon, supra, at pp. 433-434.) Rather than impose the drastic remedy of denying further discovery on this set entirely, the court continues the matter for hearing to May 16, 2025. The parties are ordered to meet and confer in person or telephonically to narrow their discovery disputes. If any disputes remain for hearing, Plaintiff shall file an updated brief of no more than 10 pages and an updated separate statement at least 9 court days in advance of the hearing. Defendant may file a responding brief of no more than 10 pages and an updated separate statement at least five court days in advance of the hearing.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (301/CVA)
